title: USA TODAY
text:
These ethical guidelines and policies aim to ensure that journalists use AI-generated content in a transparent, accurate, fair, and accountable manner. Gannett I USA TODAY I USA TODAY Network journalists are advised to follow our AI guidelines when requesting access to use and/or open AI tools and services.
Journalists must discuss the purpose and production of any AI-generated content with their editor and Gannett’s AI Council before using it. They also need an executive sponsor and approval from the AI Council for a project to move forward.
These guidelines are provided to maintain the highest standards of journalism and to maintain the trust of our audience. Gannett takes the use of AI-generated content very seriously and has put in place several safeguards to protect our intellectual property and ensure AI is used responsibly and ethically. Gannett does not use AI-generated photo-realistic images in our news coverage.
The guidelines may be revised periodically to reflect the evolving ethical implications of AI (Artificial Intelligence) technology.

title: USA TODAY
path: Accessibility Support
text:
The USA TODAY NETWORK is committed to making its desktop
site, mobile website, and mobile applications accessible for all viewers. While
we continue to develop and enhance our accessibility features, we encourage you
to explore the accessibility options installed on your device. Below is an
overview for enabling some of the accessibility options on your desktop device.
Enabling Accessibility Features (Microsoft Windows 10)
Screen Reader Support
Feature: Narrator reads text on the computer screen aloud.\n
Steps: Click Start, Control Panel, Ease of Access, Ease of Access Center, Start Narrator
Zoom Support
Feature: Magnifier allows the user to zoom in and navigate a page with higher visibility.\n
Steps: Click Start, Control Panel, Ease of Access, Optimize Visual Display, Click the checkbox next to “Turn on Magnifier”
Color Contrast Support
Feature: Windows allows you to change the contrast to what works best for you.\n
Steps: Click Start, Control Panel, Appearance and Personalization, Personalization, scroll down to Contrast Theme options and choose the best option for your needs
Navigation Support
Feature: Speech Recognition allows you to control your PC using your voice rather than a keyboard or mouse.\n
Steps: Make sure microphone is plugged in, click Start, Windows Speech Recognition (first time users complete the wizard and tutorial)
Enabling Accessibility Features (OS X El Capitan, version 10.11)
Screen Reader Support
Feature: Voiceover reads text to you and can be used to control the computer using only the keyboard
Zoom Support
Feature: Zoom allows the user to zoom in and navigate a page with higher visibility.\n
Steps: Click System Preferences on the dock, Accessibility, Zoom, then choose either of the following options:
1) To zoom with keyboard shortcuts, select "Use keyboard shortcuts to zoom."
2) To zoom with your mouse or trackpad, select "Use scroll gesture with modifier keys to zoom."
Visual Support
Feature: Under the Display options, you can alter the coloration and/or enlarge cursor size to improve visibility.\n
Steps: Click System Preferences on the dock, Accessibility, Display, then check "Invert Colors" for a black background with white font, "Use Greyscale" for a grey display, and/or "Enhance Cursor Size" to enlarge the cursor\n
Feature: Dictation and Speech reads text on the computer screen aloud.\n
Steps: Click System Preferences on the dock, Accessibility, Dictation and Speech, Text to Speech, select checkbox for “Speak selected text when the key is pressed,” choose the key/key combination you prefer (when text is highlighted and these keys are pressed the text will be read aloud)
Contact Us
With questions or feedback on accessibility features on our mobile applications, please contact accessibility@usatoday.com.

title: Aerials is a breathtaking beautiful, yet cruel, Olympic competition
text: Aerials is a breathtaking beautiful, yet cruel, Olympic competition

title: Aerials is a breathtaking beautiful, yet cruel, Olympic competition
path: Aerials is a breathtaking beautiful, yet cruel, Olympic competition
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
Peak Moments from Day 6 of Olympics
USAT's Sam Cardona-Norberg and Jordan Mendoza break down the women's short program, as Amber Glenn falls in rankings and Alysa Liu is in position to medal.
Sports Seriously
LIVIGNO, Italy — Think of it like gymnastics on skies. In that sense, aerials can be a breathtakingly beautiful Olympic event. It’s also a cruel one.
After all the training and competitions for four years, freestyle skiers perform three or four acrobatic jumps in aerials, through qualifying and finals, just for the right to perform the only one that really matters to an Olympic podium.
Winter Olympics updates: Mikaela Shiffrin comes through for slalom gold
Once competitors reach the final six, their previous scores don’t matter. It’s one jump for the medals, and in the women’s aerials Feb. 18 at Livigno Aerials & Moguls Park, it was mostly the same jump.
Of those six final skiers, two being Americans, five attempted the same triple jump – a back-full-full-full – that offered maximum difficulty.
So you'd either nail it or you wouldn’t.
In this instance, Kaila Kuhn and Winter Vinecki of Team USA did not.
The cruel part is Kuhn and Vinecki each crushed the same jump in a previous round. Their earlier scores would’ve been worth a medal if duplicated at the end. Instead, Vinecki’s final score of 90.58 (down from 107.75) meant a sixth-place finish, with Kuhn’s 99.16 (down from 109.90) one spot ahead of her in fifth.
"It’s a difficult format, for sure," Kuhn said. "… I’ve had better runs than that one, and I'm bummed I didn't put a better one down in the medal round.”
China’s Xu Mengtao won gold with a 112.90 (up from 107.75), while Australia’s Danielle Scott (102.17) took silver, and China’s Shao Qi (101.90) the bronze.
Kuhn, a 22-year-old from Michigan, improved on her eighth-place showing in aerials in Beijing in 2022. Since then, though, her career has taken off internationally. She arrived as the 2025 world champion and a solid hopeful to claim a medal in Livigno.
She almost did, too, before barely sitting down in her final landing, causing her head to snap back and the venue’s public-address announcer to groan: "She almost had it! That was so close."
“It's a privilege that I have worked so hard over these last four years that I am upset that I didn't get a medal,” said Kuhn, finding a silver lining. "Totally different mentality than last Olympics."
Vinecki was up second in the final six, and she similarly fell onto her back on the landing.
The landing isn’t everything to aerials judges. A skier’s height off the ramp and form mid-air are also heavily weighted in a jump’s assigned value. But when six of the world’s best are there trying roughly the same maneuvers, a clean landing is advisable.
"Which is kind of insane, because in the Olympics past, that hasn’t been the case where it’s the same degree of difficulty for the triples,” said Vinecki, who also improved her Beijing finish (15th). “… I think you’re going to start to see it more and more common that the girls are going to start doing full-full-full, maybe even more harder tricks in the future.”
This women's aerials competition was postponed by heavy snow this week in Livigno, with qualifying moved to the same day as the finals.
Traditionally, the United States has not been a powerhouse in this event at the Olympics. When American Megan Nick won a bronze medal at the 2022 Olympics in Beijing, it was Team USA's first women's aerials medal since 1998.
But three Americans reached the field of 12 finalists this time. Tasia Tanner placed 11th (score of 85.36).
Tanner said she works full-time as an IT manager for Hilton Hotels, working at the Waldorf Astoria in Park City, Utah. That means she has worked 40 hours a week while also training 40 hours a week.
Only 23, Tanner is getting married in April and said this may have been her final Olympics.
“I have had three surgeries on my right side. I had a labrum surgery and two ACLs,” she said. “My body is currently just not really able to keep up with this sport, unfortunately."
See?
Aerials is cruel.
A lot of fun to watch, though.
Reach sports columnist Gentry Estes at gestes@gannett.com and hang out with him on Bluesky @gentryestes.bsky.social

2026 Winter Olympics: Hockey, skating venues in Milan
Speed skating. Figure skating. Hockey. USA TODAY's Alex Connor went to Milan's three venues to see the Olympic action. This is how it went.
The United States cricket squad's run in the 2026 ICC T20 World Cup has officially come to an end.
Following Pakistan's victory over Namibia on Wednesday, Feb. 18, the U.S. was eliminated from the Group A standings, finishing in third place with a 2-2 record. It opened the tournament with back-to-back losses against India and Pakistan.
REQUIRED READING: Similarities between cricket, baseball ahead of T20 Cricket World Cup
Team USA bounced back with wins over the Netherlands and Namibia. However, with India winning four matches and Pakistan three, the U.S. finished behind the two countries it lost to due to points in the standings and in the win column.
As the top two teams from each of the four groups advance to the Super 8 round ― the round the U.S. reached in the 2024 T20 World Cup ― Pakistan and India will play on, while Team USA returns home following a strong showing.
The Super 8 phase for the T20 World Cup 2026 begins on Feb. 21 and will conclude on March 1.
Here's a look back at how Team USA performed in the 2026 T20 World Cup:

title: United States eliminated from 2026 T20 World Cup. USA stats, results
path: United States eliminated from 2026 T20 World Cup. USA stats, results > How did Team USA cricket perform in T20 World Cup?
text:
Feb. 7: India defeated the United States by 29 runs\n India defeated the United States by 29 runs
Feb. 10: Pakistan defeated the United States by 32 runs\n  Pakistan defeated the United States by 32 runs
Feb. 13: United States defeated the Netherlands by 93 runs\n  United States defeated the Netherlands by 93 runs
Feb. 15: United States defeated Namibia by 31 runs
The United States opened the tournament by giving India all it could handle, limiting the defending champions to a score of 161 with nine wickets in the full 20 overs. However, while the bowling did its job, the batting struggled against India's strong bowling attack.
Against Pakistan, the final score made the match seem closer than it was, as the USA was never competitive after Pakistan posted a 190 score on the batting side. But until the final bowl, the United States hung around.
Following the back-to-back losses, Team USA had its best batting performances against the Netherlands and Namibia. The U.S. scored 196 in 20 overs and lost just six wickets vs. the Dutch, and then the bowling attack got the Netherlands all-out for 103 runs in 15.5 overs.
Against Namibia, the U.S. bested its highest score ever in the T20 World Cup two days after setting the record with a 199 score for the loss of just four wickets. The United States used the strong batting performance to pick up a 31-run win over Namibia to both help its net-run rate and keep itself alive in the tournament.

title: United States eliminated from 2026 T20 World Cup. USA stats, results
path: United States eliminated from 2026 T20 World Cup. USA stats, results > Top Team USA performers in T20 World Cup
text:
Captain Monank Patel, Sanjay Krishnamurthi, Saiteja Mukkamalla and Shubham Ranjane each recorded a run score of 50 in a T20 World Cup match. Patel and Krishnamurthi each scored 50s in the win over Namibia. Krishnamurthi had 68 off 33 balls in that match.
Mukkamalla recorded the highest single-game score of 79 runs off 51 balls in the USA's win over the Netherlands, while Ranjane was the most consistent player with scores of 37 (off 22 balls), 51 (30) and 48 (24) over the first three games.
In the four games for the U.S., Shadley van Schalkwyk led the bowling attack with 13 wickets taken, with four-wicket performances against India and Pakistan. Harmeet Singh recorded seven wickets, while Mohammad Mohsin added four wickets.

path: Adorable dog unexpectedly runs onto Olympic cross-country course
text:
Show Caption

Wolfdog crashes Olympic ski course mid race finishing to cheers
A wolfdog named Nazgul steals the show after sprinting onto the Olympic ski course and crossing the finish line with the athletes.
With great closing speed, an unexpected entry crossed the finish line in the women's cross-country team sprint ... and the crowd in Tesero went wild.
A dog somehow managed to get on the course during the qualification round on Wednesday, Feb. 18, bounding down the final stretch, and as the exhausted skiers concluded their runs, the playful pup made a host of new friends in the finish area.
The NBC Sports commentators on the live broadcast were quick to point out the warm welcome: "The biggest cheer of the day ... and it's not for any of the skiers."
Unfortunately, the canine competitor didn't complete the entire course and was ineligible for a medal.
But some head scratches and maybe a treat will likely be even more rewarding for such an Olympian effort.

title: USA's Ben Ogden, Gus Schumacher win silver in men's cross-country team sprint
text: USA's Ben Ogden, Gus Schumacher win silver in men's cross-country team sprint

title: USA's Ben Ogden, Gus Schumacher win silver in men's cross-country team sprint
path: USA's Ben Ogden, Gus Schumacher win silver in men's cross-country team sprint
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
Jessie Diggins on her "painful day" but thrilled to compete for bronze
Team USA's cross country skiier Jessie Diggins speaks on fought through bruised ribs to compete for a bronze medal.
Sports Seriously
MILAN — Entering the 2026 Winter Olympics, Team USA had only won four cross-country skiing medals in Olympic history. The Americans have nearly matched that in Milano Cortina alone after picking up another medal.
Team USA claimed silver in the men's team sprint free on Wednesday. Ben Ogden and Gus Schumacher posted a time of 18:30.35 for the U.S. cross-country skiing team's third podium finish after Jessie Diggins claimed bronze in the women's 10km and Ogden won bronze in the individual sprint.
In team sprint, each pairing is made up of two athletes who alternate laps. In the final round, teams start at the same time and complete six total laps (three per team member). Whoever crosses the finish line first wins.
Ogden and Schumacher finished 1.37 seconds off the top pace of Norway. Italy claimed the bronze medal.
Norwegian sensation Johannes Høsflot Klæbo won another gold medal with partner Einar Hedegart with a time of 18:28.98. Klaebo has won gold in every men's cross-country event so far at the 2026 Winter Games, with one more event to go. Klaebo improves to 10 total gold medals and joins Michael Phelps (23) as the only Olympians, Summer or Winter, to reach double-digit gold medals.
This silver medal is the second of the 2026 Games for Ogden. He also captured silver in the men's sprint classic, finishing with a time of 3:40.61, less than a second behind Norwegian star Johannes Høsflot Klæbo. Norway's Oskar Opstad Vike finished with the bronze medal with a time of 3:46.55.
Ogden's silver in the classic sprint ended a nearly 50-year Olympic medal drought for the American men. He became just the second American man to win an Olympic cross-country skiing medal and the first to make the podium since Bill Koch captured silver in 30km at the 1976 Innsbruck Winter Olympics.
Now, Ogden and Schumacher have added another entry to the American history books.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
text: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
Nicki Minaj says she's not flipping on Trump support
President Donald Trump hosted a Trump Accounts summit where Nicki Minaj and other CEOs and companies pledged their support.
Trump Accounts are new investment accounts for children under 18, launching on July 5, 2026.\n
Children born between 2025 and 2028 will receive $1,000 in seed money from the federal government.\n
Parents can open an account by filing IRS Form 4547 online or with their 2025 tax return.
Tax season is well underway, and some may be wondering if their kids are eligible for a Trump Account.
Parents can open the savings accounts for American kids under the age of 18 (with a Social Security number), but only those born between 2025 and 2028 will receive the $1,000 seed money from the federal government. Still, other private donations may be eligible for kids born outside those years. For example, Michael and Susan Dell pledged $6.25 billion for the first 25 million applicants for children aged 10 and under, with some additional restrictions.
Here is what to know about the Trump Accounts and how to open one.
More: What's in a name? Plenty, when that name is 'Trump'

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > When do Trump Accounts become available?
text: Trump Accounts launch on July 5, 2026. Contributions cannot be made before that date.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > How do you open a Trump Account?
text:
Parents or legal guardians have at least two options for opening an account. The latest, and easiest, is to file IRS Form 4547 online at trumpaccounts.gov. The form will ask for dates of birth, Social Security numbers and contact information.
You can also file Form 4547 with your 2025 tax return.
Eligible families should consider filing the form sooner than later, so the account is ready for the July rollout, Vanguard advises.
After submission, the IRS will establish the Trump Account. Sometime later, “you’ll be contacted by the partner financial firm where your Trump Account will be held, with further instructions on how to complete the setup,” the official website instructs.
That process will begin in May, according to a guidance document.
One detail yet to be released is the identity of the “partner financial firm” that will serve as custodian of the accounts.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > When will the $1,000 land in your Trump Account?
text: The seed money will land in your Trump Account sometime after July 4 and “as soon as practicable” after the account is authenticated, a Treasury guidance document says.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > Who actually owns a Trump Account?
text: The Trump Account is owned by the child but administered by an adult until the child turns 18, according to Fidelity Investments.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > Who qualifies for a Trump Account?
text:
All U.S. citizens born between 2025 and 2028 qualify for Trump Accounts. You must have a Social Security number.
American children born before 2025 who are under 18 will also be eligible for Trump Accounts, with all the same features, but with no $1,000 seed money from the federal government.
Only one Trump Account may be opened per child.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > When can you withdraw the funds?
text:
On this point, the facts remain unclear.
IRS rules say you can take distributions from a Trump Account on Jan. 1 of the year you turn 18.
But that guidance may change to allow withdrawals only after the child’s 18th birthday, said Rita Assaf, vice president of retirement offerings at Fidelity. The reason: A kid who hasn’t turned 18 doesn’t have the same legal rights as an adult.
From that point, Trump Accounts will work much like Individual Retirement Accounts. IRA rules generally penalize withdrawals before age 59½, with exceptions that include a qualified first-time home purchase, higher education and medical expenses. After age 59½, you can withdraw IRA funds without penalty.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > How can you invest money in a Trump Account?
text:
Trump Accounts come with unusual restrictions. You can invest the funds only in low-cost index funds: either mutual funds or ETFs that track an index of “primarily” American stocks. At least 90% of the funds must be invested in U.S. firms, according to IRS guidance. The fund may not charge an expense ratio higher than 0.1%. Expense ratios are essentially administrative fees.
It’s a unique set of rules, with an overarching theme of “invest in America,” said J. Spencer Williams, founder and CEO of the Retirement Clearinghouse, a financial technology firm.
The rules change when the child turns 18, and the Trump Account becomes “an ordinary IRA,” Williams said. After that, account holders will have more freedom in how to invest the funds.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > Who will administer Trump Accounts?
text:
The U.S. Treasury will create and administer Trump Accounts, with the yet-unnamed “partner firm” serving as custodian.
Eventually, families will be permitted to move the accounts to outside financial providers: presumably, the same companies that already administer 401(k) and IRA accounts.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > Who can contribute to a Trump Account?
text: Contributions can come from individuals, employers, governments, philanthropists and charities: pretty much anyone.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > How much can be contributed to a Trump Account?
text:
Contributions max out at $5,000 a year. But the one-time government “seed” contribution doesn’t count toward the annual limit, nor do contributions from governments and charities.
An employer may contribute up to $2,500 per year per employee, money that may be split among multiple children. Employees can divert pre-tax pay into a Trump Account. Those contributions count toward the $5,000 annual limit.
The annual limits are indexed to inflation and will rise over time.

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > How are Trump Accounts taxed?
text:
Individual contributions to Trump Accounts generally will come from after-tax dollars. When those funds are withdrawn, only the earnings are taxed, Vanguard advises.
Contributions from other sources, including employers and employees, charities and governments, are pre-tax, so the full value of a withdrawal is taxed.
With so many potential contributors, differing tax rules and variable contribution limits, Trump Account holders may struggle to keep track.
“It’s unclear what happens if Granddad puts money in and your employer also puts money in,” Williams said. “It’s unclear how all of that is going to be sorted out over time.”

title: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know
path: Trump Accounts for kids under 10 could still get funding. What to know > Who has announced Trump Account matching funds?
text:
Philanthropists Michael and Susan Dell have pledged $6.25 billion: $250 each to the first 25 million Trump Account applicants ages 10 or under and born before 2025, from ZIP codes with median incomes under $150,000.
The Dell pledge expands the reach of Trump Accounts: “That’s not for newborns,” said Assaf of Fidelity.
Philanthropists Ray and Barbara Dalio have pledged $250 contributions to 300,000 children in Connecticut. Hedge fund manager Brad Gerstner announced a similar pledge in Indiana, part of a federal “50 State Challenge” to mobilize philanthropy nationwide. Rapper Nicki Minaj said she would donate between $150,000 and $300,000 to her fans' Trump Accounts.
JPMorgan Chase, Charles Schwab, BlackRock, Visa, Intel, Bank of America and many other companies have pledged Trump Account contributions to the children of employees.
Kinsey Crowley is the Trump Connect reporter for the USA TODAY Network. Reach her at KCrowley@usatodayco.com. Follow her on X (Twitter), Bluesky and TikTok.

text: UCLA basketball coach Mick Cronin ejects own player in lopsided loss

title: UCLA basketball coach Mick Cronin ejects own player in lopsided loss
path: UCLA basketball coach Mick Cronin ejects own player in lopsided loss
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
Top freshman college basketball players to watch ahead of March Madness
As March Madness approaches, a standout freshman class is already shaping the tournament picture. Here are four rising stars poised to make an impact on the national stage.
Things got so bad during UCLA's blowout road loss to Michigan State on Tuesday night that Bruins coach Mick Cronin apparently felt the need to eject one of his own players.
The incident occurred late in the second half of an eventual 82-59 loss on Feb. 17 when UCLA center Steven Jamerson II picked up a flagrant foul for hacking a Spartans player from behind on a dunk attempt.
Cronin explained afterward he didn't appreciate Jamerson's actions, especially coming with just 4:26 to go and the Bruins trailing 77-50.
"I was thoroughly disappointed," Cronin told reporters after the game. "The guy was defenseless in the air. I know Steve was trying to block the shot, but the game's a 25-point game. You don't do that."
Frustrations continue to mount for the Bruins, who lost to top-ranked Michigan by 30 points on Saturday after entering the weekend winning five of their last six.
Cronin also had a testy exchange with a reporter after the game. When asked about the Michigan State student section chanting the name of former Spartans player Xavier Booker − who transferred to UCLA last year after two underwhelming seasons in East Lansing − Cronin fired back angrily.
"I would like to give you kudos for the worst question I've ever been asked," he said. "You really think I care about the other team's student section?"
The loss dropped UCLA to 17-9 overall and 9-6 in the Big Ten. Michigan State improved to 21-5 and 11-4 in Big Ten play.

How to create a shared album on your iPhone
Texting and AirDrop are great ways to share photos with friends and family, but creating a shared album might be the easier option for everyone.
Problem Solved
Photos are no fun — unless you share them with everyone in them. But coordinating the sharing and distribution of lots of pictures can be tricky. Texting photos is easy, AirDropping them is even easier, but one of the simplest ways to share them is to create a shared album.
Everyone involved can upload their pictures and enjoy those taken by others — right from their phones.
Shared albums are especially useful for special events like vacations, reunions and bachelorette parties. You can also use them as your own ultra-private social media platform with friends or family. Just like on social media, photos in shared albums can be liked and commented on.

Sweden is 2-0 against the USA in the NHL era of the Winter Olympics.
The United States will try to change that history when it faces Sweden (3:10 p.m. ET, NBC, Peacock) in the quarterfinals on Wednesday, Feb. 18.
Sweden defeated the USA 4-2 in 1998 in the first Olympics to use NHL players and it also won in 2006 on its way to a gold medal.
The USA is the higher seed in 2026 in the NHL's first Olympics participation since 2014. The Americans finished 3-0 in the preliminary round and earned a bye to the quarterfinals. Those wins came against teams with fewer NHL players, so Sweden will be the United States' toughest opponent to date.
Sweden lost to NHL-stocked Finland in the round robin and is playing on back-to-back days. It beat Latvia 5-1 in the qualification round, the same score as the USA's win over the Latvians.

title: When does Jordan Stolz skate again at 2026 Winter Olympics?
path: When does Jordan Stolz skate again at 2026 Winter Olympics?
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
Speed skating legend Bonnie Blair on Jordan Stolz's quest for more gold medals
Olympic gold medalist Bonnie Blair joins Milan Magic to talk about the power of the men's speed skating team
Sports Seriously
American speed skater Jordan Stolz is already one of the defining stars to emerge from the 2026 Winter Olympics, setting Olympic records en route to winning two gold medals. But he's not done yet, with two more gold medals and a feat that hasn't been done in more than 45 years still within reach at the Milano Cortina Games.
Stolz will attempt to become the first U.S. speed skater since Eric Heiden in 1980 to win four gold medals in the same Olympics, with the men's 1,500m race and mass start still on his schedule before the 2026 Winter Olympics end. The 21-year-old already won the 500m and 1,000m speed skating events in recent days. He can further cement himself as one of the sport's all-time greats if he can come through as a favorite again in two more races.
Here's a look at when Stolz is next scheduled to race at the 2026 Winter Olympics, as well as his schedule for the rest of the Milano Cortina Games and how to watch:

text:

David Archuleta on faith identity and leaving the Mormon church
David Archuleta shares how faith, identity and LGBTQ acceptance shaped his journey after leaving the Mormon church.
Will your next great read give you the inside scoop on your favorite celebrity?
Last year’s roster of tell-alls brought stories from everyone from Malala Yousafzai to Christine Brown Woolley from “Sister Wives.” Nia Sioux told us about growing through her experience on “Dance Moms,” where she says she was subjected to racism and toxicity. Shari Franke penned a raw portrait of life under her mother, disgraced vlogger and convicted child abuser Ruby Franke. “Survivor” alum Parvati Shallow took us from her childhood in a commune to public scrutiny after her time on reality TV.
Who do we have our eye on this year? Here are 10 celebrity memoirs coming out soon.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘La Lucci’ by Susan Lucci (out now)
text: Soap opera star Lucci takes readers through her early days getting cast as Erica Kane on “All My Children” to the moment she found out, over 40 years later, that the show was canceled. But though “La Lucci” has plenty of humor and joyful anecdotes, Lucci also gets candid about grief after she lost her husband of 52 years.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘Devout’ by David Archuleta (out now)
text: Archuleta skyrocketed to fame at age 17 as the runner-up on “American Idol” Season 7. But behind the scenes, he was grappling with alleged emotional abuse from his dad. He was also coming to terms with his sexuality, but coming out as queer was at odds with his Mormon faith. In “Devout,” Archuleta shares how he lost his church to find himself.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘It's Never Too Late’ by Marla Gibbs (Feb. 24)
text: “The Jeffersons” star Gibbs is telling her story at the age of 94, starting from when she moved to Los Angeles to start anew after an abusive marriage. “It’s Never Too Late” chronicles her career in Hollywood, including her fight for fair pay and access for herself and her peers.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘You Better Believe I'm Gonna Talk About It’ by Lisa Rinna (Feb. 24)
text: If you miss her on “The Traitors,” Rinna is coming back your way soon. In “You Better Believe I’m Gonna Talk About It,” the Real Housewife and pop culture icon dishes on her career from acting on “Days of Our Lives” to Bravo's reality universe, while also getting personal on parenting, marriage and grief.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘You With the Sad Eyes’ by Christina Applegate (March 3)
text: In an honest and darkly funny memoir, Applegate is opening up about how multiple sclerosis changed her life, forcing her to confront memories of body dysmorphia, family addiction and her changing relationship with her body today. “You With the Sad Eyes” is a retrospective of the diaries Applegate has kept her whole life, promising “a story not even those closest to her fully know.”

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘Kids, Wait Till You Hear This!’ by Liza Minnelli (March 10)
text: Minnelli initially announced her memoir after claiming documentary depictions of her life “didn’t get it right.” In “Kids, Wait Till You Hear This!” Minnelli gets real about growing up as the only child of famed actor Judy Garland and director Vincente Minnelli, focusing on joy even as she was struggling with substance use disorder, chronic anxiety and financial woes.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘Phases’ by Brandy (March 31)
text: In “Phases,” actor and Grammy Award-winning singer Brandy juxtaposes a career filled with record deals and roles with the pressure she felt behind closed doors. From her start in Mississippi churches to her life in the spotlight today, this memoir charts Brandy’s journey to make peace with the past while looking toward the future.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘Famesick’ by Lena Dunham (April 14)
text: In “Famesick,” Dunham reflects on chronic illness, fame, sex and creative ambition. Behind the scenes of Dunham’s red carpet and TV appearances, there was explained pain that made her body feel “like towing a wrecked car across town at midnight.” Here, she unpacks how her diagnoses and treatment have changed the way she thinks about her work.

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘Transcendent’ by Laverne Cox (June 9)
text: “Transcendent” is a journey through Cox’s history making career as one of Hollywood’s most high-profile transgender actors, as well as what she overcame to get there. From childhood trauma and depression to working at a New York City drag restaurant to booking “Orange is the New Black,” Cox’s memoir promises readers can “heal and fight for equality, right alongside Laverne.”

title: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone
path: 10 can't-miss celebrity memoirs, from Christina Applegate to Sylvester Stallone > ‘The Steps’ by Sylvester Stallone (Sept. 29)
text:
In Stallone’s anticipated memoir, his famous run up the Philadelphia Museum of Art steps serves as a metaphor for the steps he took to pursue his dreams. Stallone chronicles his difficult childhood and the period of time between arriving in New York City in 1969 and sweeping the 49th Academy Awards in 1977 for “Rocky.”
Clare Mulroy is USA TODAY’s Books Reporter, where she covers buzzy releases, chats with authors and dives into the culture of reading. Find her on Instagram, subscribe to our weekly Books newsletter or tell her what you’re reading at cmulroy@usatoday.com.

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
text: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
path: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
text:
Although DNA evidence collected in the Nancy Guthrie investigation has yet to yield matches, law enforcement is still bracing for results that could expose cracks in the southern Arizona case garnering national attention.
As the search for "Today" anchor Savannah Guthrie's missing mother has stretched into its third week, the Pima County Sheriff's Department and the FBI continue to pursue various leads, review surveillance footage and analyze DNA evidence recovered at and near the 84-year-old's home outside Tucson, Arizona.
Authorities have not named a person of interest as of Tuesday, Feb. 17, but are seeking to identify a suspect shown in doorbell camera footage at Guthrie's front door, released on Feb. 10. The suspect is described as a "male, approximately 5’9”-5’10” tall, with an average build."
Gloves found in a field near a road appear to match those worn by the suspect in the doorbell camera video, the FBI confirmed to USA TODAY on Feb. 15.
Here's what to know about the DNA evidence collected, what it has revealed so far and what results authorities are awaiting.

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
path: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending > What DNA has been collected in Savannah Guthrie missing mom case?
text:
Among the DNA recovered at the scene were dried blood droplets spotted on the Spanish tile entrance at the home on Feb. 3, which the Pima County Sheriff's Department later confirmed was Nancy Guthrie's blood.
The sheriff revealed on Feb. 13 that investigators collected DNA from the 84-year-old's home that did not belong to her or anyone in close contact with her.
Around 16 gloves have been collected near the Guthrie house, the FBI confirmed, while clarifying that most "were searchers' gloves discarded in various areas when they searched the vicinity." Gloves closest to the property, approximately 2 miles away, were sent for testing.

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
path: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending > Who is analyzing the DNA?
text:
DNA evidence in the Nancy Guthrie investigation has been sent to DNA Labs International, a private lab in South Florida.
Pima County Sheriff Chris Nanos has shut down speculation of friction between local authorities and the FBI, prompted by a Feb. 12 Reuters report citing a "U.S. law enforcement official with knowledge of the case" about a dispute over where to send DNA samples.
In an interview with NBC affiliate KVOA published Friday, Feb. 13, Nanos confirmed he had disagreed with the bureau's attempt to send "one or two" gloves to its lab close by, but said the FBI eventually agreed that it made more "sense" to centralize the evidence at the Florida lab. "There's no arguing there. There's no fight. Sheriff didn't block anything. This has always been a case where we both work very close together."

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
path: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending > What DNA results have been found in Nancy Guthrie case?
text:
The Pima County Sheriff's Department confirmed Tuesday, Feb. 17, that preliminary results failed to match the DNA collection at her home to the gloves the FBI said resembled those of the suspect seen in the surveillance video.
The DNA profile also did not match anyone in the FBI's Combined DNA Index System (CODIS), the sheriff's department said in a Feb. 17 news release.
"Investigators are currently looking into additional investigative genetic genealogy options for DNA evidence to check for matches," the news release said. "CODIS is one option of many databases that are available."

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
path: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending > What DNA results are pending?
text:
Following the preliminary results released Tuesday, the FBI is now "awaiting quality control and official confirmation today before putting 'unknown male profile'" into its national database.
Investigators are also awaiting the results for additional DNA evidence found at Nancy Guthrie's home, which did not belong to the missing mother or her inner circle.

title: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending
path: Nancy Guthrie DNA findings so far as key results remain pending > What's next in DNA analysis?
text:
In a Feb. 17 interview with NBC News, Nanos said the lack of a match from the DNA found on the gloves is "not the end."
"Now we start with genealogy and some of the partial DNA we have at the home," he told the outlet. "To me, that’s more critical than any glove I found 2 miles away. I’m not dismissing the glove 2 miles away, but I have gloves 5 miles away, 10 miles away, so we prioritize."
He continued: "We believe that we may have some DNA there that may be our suspect, but we won’t know that until that DNA is separated, sorted out, maybe admitted to CODIS, maybe through genealogy."

title: From coma to Olympic medal, how USA's Jake Canter shocked everyone in men's slopestyle
text: From coma to Olympic medal, how USA's Jake Canter shocked everyone in men's slopestyle

title: From coma to Olympic medal, how USA's Jake Canter shocked everyone in men's slopestyle
path: From coma to Olympic medal, how USA's Jake Canter shocked everyone in men's slopestyle
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
Snow pummels Livigno, forces snowboarding to reschedule at Olympics
USA TODAY Sports' Chris Bumbaca braves the elements to update us on the snowy conditions in Livigno where a handful of events have been rescheduled.
Sports Seriously
LIVIGNO, Italy – When Jake Canter finally woke up from a coma, after six days, the then 13-year-old boy asked his mother a simple question.
“Am I alive?”
Somehow, despite doctors giving him a 20% chance to live, he was. A freak accident at a trampoline park in his native Colorado left his skull fractured in four places.
OK, next question.
"When can I snowboard again?”
Less than 10 years, three brain surgeries, a serious bout with depression and other health issues later, Jake Canter took his place on the podium with a bronze medal in men’s slopestyle snowboarding at the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics.
"I want to make 13-year-old me proud, and I kept saying that to myself as well,” Canter said. “I hope I made him proud. This gold’s a little dirty, but it will do.”
Canter was cross-eyed when he woke up from his coma. He had to relearn how to walk and talk.
"It’s crazy to be standing here with a medal around my neck,” he said.
The fashion in which he earned bronze could also be described as “crazy.” Canter secured his place on Team USA by winning the Aspen World Cup five weeks ago with a massive 1980 jump to lift him into first place. He’d need it again.
Canter saw his score flash on the video after the first run and determined the judges, clearly, wanted to see more out of him. His coach said he’d have to up his run by doing a double-cork out of the “uprail” on the first portion of the course. The only problem was that he hadn’t done that yet. Comfort filled his veins anyway. Canter fell trying that move on his second run, but the mistake actually gave him confidence and informed his adjustments for the third run.
“He’s dealt with so much, injuries and stuff like that,” said U.S. teammate Red Gerard, who finished sixth. “I think out of the U.S. team, he’s probably worked the hardest in the last four years out of any of us. I think it’s refreshing to see it be earned.”
The 22-year-old seamlessly executed the rail portion, which included a double flip 900 out of the last rail, and headed to his jumps. There was the switchside double-cork 1440 for the first jump, another switch ride for a 1260 and the now-patented backside 1980 for the grand finale.
Canter stomped his final two jumps and raised both arms – half disbelief, half celebration. Later, he struggled to describe his emotions. He couldn’t believe it, couldn’t be happier. His best finish and lone appearance at a world championship was 11th, in 2023, and he was making his Olympic debut.
“Just excitement,” he said. “I knew I could do it. Just pushing myself to that level, to really go and do those tricks and that run, just made everything feel insane.”
China’s Su Yiming won his fourth career Olympic medal (and second gold) and Japan’s Taiga Hasegawa took silver.
A few months after being released from the hospital, Canter returned in even worse condition. Spinal fluid leaked through the fractures – he never experienced brain bleeding, luckily – and resulted in bacterial meningitis. Snowboarding videos comforted him and gave him something to look forward to during those multiple visits and weeks spent bedridden in a hospital.
On Wednesday, standing at the top of the mountain before his final run, Canter thought about how the pressure was a privilege. He looked at the crowd. He thought about his family and everyone supporting him, the ups and downs mentally and physically over the years, whether it was injuries or depression. He zoomed out inside that brain that somehow was still intact, still serving him well.
“I was in a coma when I was 13,” Canter said to himself. "I probably shouldn’t be doubting myself as much as I am. Stop thinking. Just enjoy the moment.”
Now in third place, Canter had to wait for nine competitors – seven who could overtake him – to finish their runs. He looks up to those guys, the same ones trying to knock him off the podium. Canada’s Mark McMorris, Canter’s snowboarding hero whom he imitated as a youngster, took another hard fall after injuring himself during big air qualifying at the start of the Games. The wait for Marcus Kleveland of Norway’s score to be posted took an eternity – and even longer for Canter, who crouched at one of the Olympic-logo walls at the bottom of the course and couldn’t bear to watch. Dane Menzies, of New Zealand, is a friend of Canter’s and finished as the top qualifier, which meant he had the last run of the day. When he fell, that meant Canter’s place on the podium, finally, was secure.
"I want them all to ride at their best. We’re all pushing each other to ride at our best,” he said. “It was really difficult. Because I want them to have theirs, too. But I have to be a little selfish, and I want mine as well. It’s pretty unreal to be holding this in my hand right now.”
That would be the piece of bronze that is now forever his. During the medalists’ news conference, he could hardly take his eyes off his medal, every inch, front and back, examined again and again.
As he agonized during the judges’ deliberations for Kleveland’s run, Canter called his girlfriend, Lexi Trufasu, to help calm his nerves. She told him to breathe. They crossed their fingers and their toes together. Medal fate was out of their control. Kleveland’s 78.96 was .40 away from tying Canter.
"Honestly, it makes me hungry for more,” he said, “I’d really like to get a gold now.”
It was tense, Trufasu said, but something in her bones told her Canter would hang on.
“They were teasing us,” Trufasu told USA TODAY Sports. "I grew like three gray hairs in the time I was waiting.
"My big toe is numb. I’m insanely proud. He deserves all of this and more. He’s gone through so much. He deserves all of it.”
Trufasu said that the night before the final, laying in bed, Canter studied his run over and over. He lives for snowboarding. Breathes it. Dreams it. Consumes it. He treats it like his job.
"He was bred for snowboarding,” she said.
Canter said this season was the most fun he’s ever had while riding, a welcomed rediscovery for the Red Bull athlete. He’d lost sight of the privilege of his position, his occupation, his passion.
And snowboarding saved Canter from himself in some ways. When the stranglehold of depression suffocated him, snowboarding gave him purpose.
"The freedom that it gives you, the control, it’s all up to you,” Canter said. "I feel like I express myself a lot through my snowboarding. I feel like I’m more myself when I’m on my snowboard as well.”
Canter’s accident that left him comatose resulted in him going deaf in his right ear. So say it with some gusto. Scream it. Shout it. It’s a story worth listening to and remembering.
Jake Canter, Olympic bronze medalist.

title: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe
text: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe Skechers just dropped its lightest, fastest daily trainer to date—and runners are taking notice.

title: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe
path: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe > Skechers just dropped its lightest, fastest daily trainer to date—and runners are taking notice.
text:
Skechers is expanding its performance running lineup with the launch of the Skechers Aero Razor, a new ultra‑lightweight daily trainer designed for runners who want speed, comfort and efficiency in a single shoe.
Inspired by the Skechers Go Run Razor 3 from 2018, the 2026 Aero Razor for men and women blends a stripped‑down racing feel with modern cushioning and stability features, making it one of the brand’s lightest road running shoes to date.
Shop the Skechers Aero Razor shoe

title: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe
path: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe > What makes the Skechers Aero Razor unique?
text:
The new Skechers Aero Razor running shoe is creating buzz for its lightweight design, cushioned feel and everyday comfort. Here’s what makes it stand out.
At the core is Hyper Burst Pro Super Critical Foam, a responsive midsole designed to deliver a lightweight, energetic ride. Skechers pairs that cushioning with a patented forefoot H‑wing, which helps create a snappy toe‑off during uptempo runs and workouts.
The shoe also features Hyper Arc technology, which adapts to a runner’s stride to promote efficiency from landing through toe‑off, along with Arch Fit technology, which is a podiatrist‑certified insole system developed using extensive foot‑scan data.
More: The ultimate shopping guide for best runner-approved sneakers

title: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe
path: Meet the newest lightweight Skechers Aero Razor running shoe > Lightweight design meets stability and traction
text:
One of the biggest highlights of the Skechers Aero Razor is its weight (or lack thereof).
The shoe tips the scales at 6.9 ounces in a men’s size 9 and 5.6 ounces in a women’s size 7, placing it firmly in the lightweight performance category. With a 36mm heel, 32mm forefoot stack height and a 4mm drop, the Razor is designed for quick turnover without sacrificing protection underfoot.
For enhanced durability and grip, Skechers uses a Goodyear Performance Rubber outsole (yes, that Goodyear), engineered to provide better traction and stability on roads and pavement.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
text: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
'I'll always believe,' Tigers coach Penny Hardaway says
Memphis basketball coach Penny Hardaway speaks to reporters after his team's Feb. 1 home loss to Tulane.
ACC features a number of hot seats, including Adrian Autry and Jeff Capel.\n
Will Lane Kiffin remain the only new coach at LSU? Matt McMahon in trouble.\n
Penny Hardaway follows a good season with a really bad one at Memphis.
Jerome Tang called his Kansas State basketball team "embarrassing." No argument on that point. K-State fans wore paper bags over their heads. Embarrassed by all of it, the university fired Tang.
Tang won't be the last college basketball coach fired these next several weeks. The hot seat steams from Syracuse to LSU to Oklahoma and lands in between.
Who is out already? College basketball coach firings, changes
March Sadness: College basketball's 10 most disappointing teams

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Adrian Autry, Syracuse
text: Why it’s hot: Syracuse is mired in a yearslong nosedive. Never mind the glory days, is it too much to ask the Orange to make the NCAA Tournament? No, it shouldn’t be too much to ask. It might be time for Syracuse to break with the Jim Boeheim coaching tree.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Jeff Capel, Pittsburgh
text: Why it’s hot: This is Capel’s eighth season. He’s been to the NCAA Tournament once. This season has been a disaster, including losses to Hofstra and Quinnipiac. If Pitt keeps Capel, take that as a sign it’s too poor to pay his buyout. There’s no other case for retention.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Jake Diebler, Ohio State
text: Why it’s hot: On one hand, Diebler’s buyout would be a de minimis housekeeping cost for a revenue behemoth like Ohio State. On the other hand, the Buckeyes are on the bubble for the Big Dance. If they go dancing, there’s probably nothing to see here. If not, stay tuned.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Kim English, Providence
text: Why it’s hot: Providence became an NCAA Tournament regular under Ed Cooley, English’s predecessor. With English, the Friars are a Big East doormat. A recent loss to St. John’s included a brawl, the latest embarrassing moment in a bad season.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Steve Forbes, Wake Forest
text: Why it’s hot: Forbes won 25 games in his second season. An affable coach, he’s delivered some decent years and good soundbites. Eventually, though, every coach needs an NCAA bid. Forbes, now in Year 6, hasn’t gotten Wake Forest there.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Earl Grant, Boston College
text: Why it’s hot: Grant isn’t the sole problem at Boston College. This program lost its way almost 15 years ago and hasn’t found its way back. But Grant hasn’t been the solution, either. This bleak season includes a loss to Central Connecticut. That's the definition of a call to action.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Penny Hardaway, Memphis
text: Why it’s hot: Hardaway is following a good year (he won 29 games last season) with one of his worst. Memphis probably would hesitate before firing one of its own, but Hardaway knows as well as anyone this program has standards. He’s not meeting them.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Bobby Hurley, Arizona State
text: Why it’s hot: It’s never a good sign when a coach admits he’s “failing.” Hurley offered that brutal assessment after a loss in January. Well, you said it, coach. Prep the buyout cannon, but hold off on firing after ASU’s upset of Texas Tech.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Matt McMahon, LSU
text: Why it’s hot: McMahon was great at Murray State. He’s gone splat at LSU. If LSU wants to be an “everything school,” it must fix its basketball program. What better time than with a new athletic director, new president and new board of supervisors chairman? Would Will Wade listen to a "strong-ass offer"?

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Wes Miller, Cincinnati
text: Why it’s hot: Bob Huggins and Mick Cronin set a high bar for Cincinnati. Miller isn’t meeting the standard through five seasons. Cincinnati isn’t the type of program that’s OK with going 0-for-5 in NCAA bids under the same coach.

title: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble
path: College basketball hot seat: 13 coaches who may be in trouble > Porter Moser, Oklahoma
text: Why it’s hot: Like his SEC counterpart McMahon, Moser thrived at a mid-major but fizzled in the Power Four ranks. A tale as old as peach baskets. Oklahoma endured a nine-game losing streak earlier this winter. That’s the foundation for a firing.

title: Daily Briefing: A Georgia teacher's death
path: Daily Briefing: A Georgia teacher's death > Who is Linda Davis?
text:
Linda Davis, a Georgia schoolteacher, was killed after police say a man fleeing immigration officers crashed a car into her vehicle.
The man, 38-year-old Guatemala native Oscar Vasquez Lopez, is accused of hitting 52-year-old Linda Davis’ car on Feb. 16 outside of the Hesse K-8 School where she worked. The crash occurred as he tried to flee Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers in Savannah, Georgia, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security said. Vasquez Lopez was arrested and taken to the hospital with minor injuries, DHS said in a news release.
The incident comes amid continued national tensions over ICE's presence in U.S. cities. DHS Assistant Secretary for Public Affairs Tricia McLaughlin said in the news release that Davis’ death “is an absolute tragedy and deadly consequence of politicians and the media constantly demonizing ICE officers and encouraging those here illegally to resist arrest."

Twenty-five years ago — on Feb. 18, 2001 — the Man in Black suffered his tragic fate on the final lap of the Daytona 500. The sport of stock car racing has never been the same.
Now, you can remind yourself and also show others why generations of NASCAR fans consider Dale Earnhardt to be the great racer of all time. The Daytona Beach News-Journal has crafted two tributes to the Intimidator, who would have turned 75 in April.
The first is a coffee-table collector’s book about a champion whose legend only has continued to grow: “EARNHARDT! The Intimidator’s life, legacy and enduring popularity after 75 years.”
Buy our Earnhardt tribute book now!
The second is a special newspaper edition that documents Earnhardt’s storied career and enduring legacy.
Buy our Earnhardt commemorative edition!
At 160 pages, the hardcover book provides flag-to-flag coverage of  Earnhardt’s long run as NASCAR’s leading man, with tons of classic photos and plenty of stories — old and new.

The eight-page edition will be printed on heavier stock and will not be sold in stores. It includes stunning photos and a commemorative page print. The hardcover book and sturdy edition will make great gifts for the racing fans in your life. And because Moms, Dads and Grads season will be upon us soon, why not check something off the to-do list early, eh?

text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
November 15 marks one year since the Mike Tyson vs. Jake Paul showdown
USA TODAY Sports' Josh Peter reflects on the viral fight between Mike Tyson and Jake Paul.
Ronda Rousey is making her return back to the ring in nearly a decade for a fight that has been long desired and is now finally happening.
Most Valuable Promotions (MVP) and Netflix announced that the streaming platform would host and air a mixed martial arts event on May 16 headlined by the former UFC champion and Olympic medalist Ronda Rousey (12-2, 9 submissions, 3 KOs) against MMA legend Gina Carano (7-1, 1 submission, 3 KOs).
The bout, planned for the Intuit Dome in Inglewood, California, checks off a couple of firsts for both Netflix and the promotion group. It marks Netflix's first-ever live MMA event, as well as the inaugural MMA event for MVP.
“Been waiting so long to announce this,” Rousey told Netflix. “Me and Gina Carano are gonna throw down in the biggest superfight in women’s combat sport history, and we’re partnering with the fighter-first promoter, MVP, as well as the baddest streamer on the planet, Netflix. This is for all MMA fans past, present, and future.”
The fight will be held inside of a hexagon cage at the 18,300-max capacity Intuit Dome. Netflix will stream the event live at no additional cost to subscribers.
Carano herself is anticipating the fight, honored that Rousey selected her for her comeback to the cage.
"Ronda came to me and said there is only one person she would make a comeback for, and it’s been her dream to make this fight happen between us," Carano said to Netflix. "She thanked me for opening up doors for her career and was respectful in asking for this fight to happen.
"This is an honor. I believe I will walk out of this fight with the win, and I anticipate it will not come easy, which I welcome. This is as much for Ronda and I as it is for the fans and mixed martial arts community. What a time to be alive.”
Here's everything you need to know leading up to the fight:

The Rousey-Carano fight is a professionally sanctioned fight under the Unified Rules of MMA. It will include five rounds, five minutes each. Fighters will use four-ounce gloves. Their fight will count as a featherweight bout at 145 pounds.
They will be using an array of fighting styles and techniques from different martial arts disciplines. Styles can include but are not limited to boxing, jiujitsu, wrestling, karate and judo.

path: MMA legends Ronda Rousey, Gina Carano land Netflix fight. Here's when > Who is Ronda Rousey?
text:
Rousey made her mark on the MMA world during a record five-year span from 2011 to 2016. Her 12-2 record includes nine submissions and three knockouts, many of which victories came within the first minute of the fight.
She became an Olympic medalist in judo. Rousey shocked the world leaving MMA behind for wrestling. She helped reshape WWE's women division, building towards its first WrestleMania event.
Rousey is also a New York Times bestselling author. She's also had her hand in acting, appearing in both Fast and the Furious and The Expendables franchise screenfilms.

title: MMA legends Ronda Rousey, Gina Carano land Netflix fight. Here's when
path: MMA legends Ronda Rousey, Gina Carano land Netflix fight. Here's when > Who is Gina Carano?
text:
Carano is considered an MMA pioneer in the women's division. She is one of the first combat sports stars to cross over into the entertainment industry.
Carano blossomed after turning heads with her tenacity in the cage. Between 2006 and 2009, she posed a 7-1 record with a submission and three knockouts under her belt. Additionally in 2009, she headlined the first major MMA main event to feature two women in 2009, a landmark moment for the sport.
Carano was referred to as the "Face of Women's MMA" from her marketability and appeal outside of the cage. She landed major roles in "Haywire," "Fast & Furious 6," "Deadpool" and "The Mandalorian," helping to pave the way for others such as Rousey to follow in her footsteps.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
text: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
text:
Show Caption
Hide Caption
USA men's hockey GM Bill Guerin on leadership, fighting and bonding
USA men's hockey general manager Bill Guerin, Feb. 7, 2026 in Milan.
The NHL draft is a fluid process.
Nothing ever really stays the same, and from team to team, opinions on players vary widely.
There is a group of players every year that rise up boards, however. This year, that ranges from players who have put themselves in the top-10 conversation to prospects who have proven they are worth consideration in the first round.
Prospects can rise up draft lists because of elite-level play or a physical tool kit that oozes potential. A prospect improving through the latter half of the season tantalizes NHL teams looking to select the hot hand
Let's dive into 10 of the names who are rising up the draft boards.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Jukurit (Finland) defenseman Alberts Smits
text: It's been said a million times at this point, but the rise of Alberts Smits has been impressive beyond belief. He's the closest thing to Moritz Seider in his draft year, considering he's a big, mobile defender who showcases two-way dominance at every level. Smits could be a legitimate top-five prospect in this draft class after starting the year as a potentially intriguing prospect going outside of the first round. He had two points in four games for Latvia at the Olympics.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Djurgarden (Sweden) center Viggo Bjorck
text: If Bjorck hadn't proven over the last two months that he's a legitimate center prospect, he probably would have faced a much tougher climb up draft boards. Bjorck was a very reliable center at the world juniors and has been a top-six center in the Swedish League. In a weak year for centers, he's becoming a topic of conversation among the top pivots in the class.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Boston University (NCAA) left wing Oscar Hemming
text: After not playing hockey for the first few months of the season, Hemming joined the Boston University Terriers and immediately started to tear it up. Hemming has brought the scoring touch he's been known for, along with a physical edge. His pace has been immediately noticeable against college competition, and his eight points in 12 games have been well above expectations.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Peterborough (OHL) left wing Adam Novotny
text: Novotny's impressive transition ability, dual-threat offensive game and sound two-way play have taken a bit to garner recognition, but he has started to show up in the top 20 on many draft boards. Novotny is a well-rounded player who impressed at the world juniors despite only recording three assists. He may not have the offensive upside of some players around the top half of the first round, but he has a very clear floor to build on.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > U.S. NTDP (USHL) left wing Wyatt Cullen
text: An impressive USHL prospects game helped get more attention on him, but Cullen has been a catalyst offensively for the USA Hockey National Team Development Program. His speed and skill make him a gamebreaker at times. The NTDP doesn't have many true difference-makers, but Cullen has emerged throughout this season. His scoring totals aren't quite as high as we are used to seeing in the top NTDP players, but he paces the squad this year.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Prince George (WHL) defenseman Carson Carels
text: It was impressive enough that Carels made the world junior squad for Team Canada, but he has shown a well-rounded game in the WHL. He's physically mature and shows a willingness to get involved physically along the boards. Carels isn't a silky smooth puck-mover, but he's a rock-solid two-way player who is showing himself capable of more as the season progresses.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Quebec (QMJHL) center Maddox Dagenais
text: Dagenais is a big center with a physical edge and a willingness to play simple hockey. He has been throwing big reverse hits, getting in on the forecheck and playing dump-and-chase hockey while attacking the net front once he has possession in the offensive zone. Dagenais plays the game like an NHLer in a lot of ways, which isn't typical coming out of the QMJHL. With so few centers in this year's class, he could easily fly up boards.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Miami (NCAA) center Ilya Morozov
text: Last year, Morozov had a perfectly good season in the USHL. He was a solid forward who showed flashes of skill. When he got to the NCAA, expectations were fairly low, given he is one of the youngest players in the draft class and the youngest in college hockey. That said, he's been a top power-play threat, a penalty-killer and a top-six center consistently throughout this season. Although he's not playing the highest level of competition, he's been excelling at every turn.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > Lulea (Sweden) center Casper Juustovaara Karlsson
text: There are very few players who play with the edge, the motor and the relentless pace of Casper Juustovaara Karlsson. The undersized forward plays north-south hockey, driving the net. He doesn't shy away from physical play either and oftentimes invites it. Not every team will be able to get by his 5-foot-9 size, but if they can, they'll get one of the hardest workers in the draft.

title: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class
path: 10 biggest risers in the 2026 NHL Draft class > London (OHL) left wing Jaxon Cover
text: After building up his skill level and creativity while dominating the roller hockey circuits, Cover made the jump to ice hockey full-time, and he's one of the London Knights' scoring leaders. Cover is a highly skilled yet very raw prospect who has only been playing ice hockey for about half a decade. With the growth he's shown already, the sky is the limit for the silky-skilled left winger.
